name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَمَنْ حَآجَّكَ فِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ فَقُلْ تَعَالَوْا۟ نَدْعُ أَبْنَآءَنَا وَأَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَنِسَآءَنَا وَنِسَآءَكُمْ وَأَنفُسَنَا وَأَنفُسَكُمْ ثُمَّ نَبْتَهِلْ فَنَجْعَل لَّعْنَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٦١)
```

```english
Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you, say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our souls and your souls, then let us pray earnestly, and call down Allah’s curse upon the liars.’ (61)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْقَصَصُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٦٢)
```

```english
This is indeed the true account, for sure. There is no god but Allah, and indeed Allah is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (62)
```

```arabic
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٦٣)
```

```english
But if they turn away, indeed Allah knows best the agents of corruption. (63)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** فمن حاجك فيه من بعد ما جائك من العلم (Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you). The letter *fa* (ف) denotes derivation (التفريع); it derives the command for mutual imprecation (المباهلة) — *the act of two parties calling down the curse of Allah upon whichever of them is lying* — from the divine instruction provided through the exhaustive explanation regarding the matter of Jesus, son of Mary (peace be upon them both), along with the emphasis added at its conclusion in His saying: الحق من ربك فلا تكن من الممترين (This is the truth from your Lord, so do not be among the skeptics). The pronoun in His saying: فيه (concerning him) refers either to Jesus or to the "truth" mentioned in the previous verse.

The preceding explanation from Him, the Exalted—despite being a divine exposition in which there is no doubt—contained a radiant proof (البرهان) indicated by His saying: إن مثل عيسى عند الله كمثل آدم (Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam...) the verse. Thus, the knowledge (العلم) attained therein is knowledge from the perspective of proof as well. For this reason, its effect encompassed the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and everyone else who heard it. Even if one were to assume hesitation on the part of the arguing listener regarding the explanation being a divine revelation (وحي إلهي) in which doubt is impermissible, it would still be impermissible to doubt it from the perspective of it being a proof (برهان) grasped by the sound intellect. Perhaps it is for this reason that it was said: من بعد ما جائك من العلم (after the knowledge that has come to you) and it was not said: "after what We have explained to them."

There is another subtle point here: reminding him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) of the "knowledge" serves to comfort his noble soul, assuring him that he is the victor by the permission of Allah, and that his Lord is his helper and will never forsake him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فقل تعالوا ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم ونسائنا ونسائكم وأنفسنا وأنفسكم (say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our souls and your souls’). The first-person plural speaker in His saying: ندع (let us call) is different from the "us" in His saying: أبنائنا (our sons), نسائنا (our women), and وأنفسنا (our souls). In the first instance, it refers to the collective of both disputing parties from the sides of Islam and Christianity. In the second instance and what follows it, it refers specifically to the side of Islam. Therefore, the speech carries the meaning of: "Let us call the sons, the women, and the souls; so we shall call our sons, our women, and our souls, and you shall call your sons, your women, and your souls." Thus, there is a subtle brevity (إيجاز) in the speech.

Although the mutual imprecation (المباهلة) and mutual cursing (الملاعنة) appear outwardly as a disputation between the Messenger of Allah and the men of the Christians, the invitation was made general to include sons and women. This was to more effectively demonstrate the inviter's confidence in the truth of his claim and his being upon the truth, due to what Allah the Glorified has placed in the human heart of love and compassion for them. One sees a person shielding them with his own life and braving horrors and perils for their sake, in the path of protecting them, out of protective honor (الغيرة) and defense (الذب) for them. For this very reason, the sons were mentioned before the women, because a person's love for them is more intense and enduring.

From this, the fallacy of what some commentators have mentioned becomes apparent: that the meaning of His saying: ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم (let us call our sons and your sons) etc., is "we shall call your sons, your women, and your souls, and you shall call our sons, our women, and our souls." This is because it nullifies the reason we mentioned for including the sons and women in the mutual imprecation.

In the detailed enumeration, there is another indication of the inviter's reliance and dependence upon the truth. It is as if he is saying: "Let the group imprecate against the group, and let both groups place the curse of Allah upon the liars, so that the curse and punishment encompass the sons, the women, and the souls. By this, the remnants of the stubborn shall be cut off, and the root of the followers of falsehood shall be uprooted."

Through this, it becomes clear that the truthfulness of the speech does not depend on a plurality of sons, nor a plurality of women, nor a plurality of souls. The ultimate objective is for one of the two parties to perish along with those with him, young and old, male and female. The commentators are in unanimous agreement, the narrations are in consensus, and history supports that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) attended the mutual imprecation and no one was present with him except Ali, Fatimah, and the two Hasans (peace be upon them). Thus, only two "souls," two "sons," and one "woman" attended it, and the command of Allah the Glorified was fulfilled through them.

Furthermore, the meaning of the wording of the verse is one thing, and the external instance (المصداق) to which the ruling applies is another. It is frequent in the Quran that a ruling, promise, or threat is addressed to a group (الجماعة), while its external instance according to the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) is a single individual. Examples include His saying, the Exalted: الذين يظاهرون منكم من نسائهم ما هن أمهاتهم (As for those of you who repudiate their wives by *zihar*, they are not their mothers...) [Al-Mujadilah: 2], and His saying, the Exalted: والذين يظاهرون من نسائهم ثم يعودون لما قالوا (Those who repudiate their wives by *zihar* and then retract what they have said...) [Al-Mujadilah: 3], and His saying, the Exalted: لقد سمع الله قول الذين قالوا إن الله فقير ونحن أغنياء (Allah has certainly heard the remarks of those who said, ‘Allah is poor and we are rich’...) [Al-Imran: 181], and His saying, the Exalted: ويسألونك ماذا ينفقون قل العفو (They ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘The surplus’...) [Al-Baqarah: 219]; among many other verses that were revealed in the plural form while their instance according to the occasion of revelation was singular (مفرد).

**His saying, the Exalted:** ثم نبتهل فنجعل لعنة الله علی الكاذبين (then let us pray earnestly and call down Allah’s curse upon the liars). Earnest prayer (الابتهال) is derived from *al-bahla* (البهلة), which means the curse (اللعنة). This is its root; then its usage became frequent for supplication (الدعاء) and petition (المسألة) when accompanied by persistence and urgency.

His saying: فنجعل لعنة الله (and call down Allah’s curse) serves as an explanation for the earnest prayer. It was said: فنجعل (and let us place/call down) rather than "and let us ask," to indicate that it is a supplication that will not be rejected, whereby the truth is distinguished from falsehood through a process of dependency and consequence.

His saying: الكاذبين (the liars) is used in the sense of specific reference (العهد) rather than total exhaustion (الاستغراق) or the genus (الجنس). The intent is not to place the curse upon every liar or the genus of liars, but rather upon the liars involved in one of the two sides of the disputation occurring between him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and the Christians, where he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: "Indeed, Allah, there is no god but He, and indeed Jesus is His servant and His messenger," while they said: "Indeed Jesus is God," or "he is the son of God," or "indeed Allah is the third of three."

Based on this, it is clear that if the claim and the mutual imprecation regarding it were between the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and the Christians—meaning one side was an individual and the other side was a group—it would have been necessary to express it with a term that could apply to both the singular and the plural, such as saying: "and let us place the curse of Allah upon whoever is a liar." Thus, the speech indicates the realization of "liars" in the plural description in one of the two sides of the disputation and imprecation in any case: either on the side of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) or on the side of the Christians. This implies that those present for the imprecation were partners in the claim (الدعوى), for lying only occurs in a claim. Therefore, those who were present with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم)—namely Ali, Fatimah, and the two Hasans (peace be upon them)—share in the claim and the invitation (الدعوة) with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). This is among the most excellent virtues (المناقب) with which Allah specifically honored the People of the House (أهل البيت) of His Prophet (peace be upon them), just as He specifically designated them as the "souls," "women," and "sons" for His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) from among the men, women, and sons of the nation (الامة).

**If you say:** It has already been mentioned that the Quran frequently uses the plural form for a singular instance, and that the use of "women" in the verse—while the one who was present from them for the imprecation was restricted to Fatimah (peace be upon her)—is an example of this. What then prevents the usage of the term "the liars" (الكاذبين) in this same manner?

**I say:** There is a difference between the two cases. The use of the plural form in verses for a singular instance is because the reality (الحقيقة) being explained is something that is capable of being realized by many, which necessitates their inclusion in the ruling of the verse. However, in cases where this is not permissible because the subject of the verse is something that the ruling cannot transcend, and the description cannot encompass others, then there is no doubt that it is impermissible. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: وإذ تقول للذي أنعم الله عليه وأنعمت عليه أمسك عليك زوجك واتق الله (When you said to him whom Allah had blessed and whom you [too] had blessed, ‘Retain your wife for yourself, and be wary of Allah’...) [Al-Ahzab: 37], and His saying, the Exalted: لسان الذي يلحدون إليه أعجمي وهذا لسان عربي مبين (The tongue of him they hint at is outlandish, while this is a clear Arabic tongue) [Al-Nahl: 103], and His saying, the Exalted: إنا أحللنا لك أزواجك اللاتي آتيت أجورهن (O Prophet! Indeed We have made lawful to you your wives whom you have given their dowries...) until His saying: وامرأة مؤمنة إن وهبت نفسها للنبي إن أراد النبي أن يستنكحها خالصة لك من دون المؤمنين (...and a faithful woman if she offers herself to the Prophet and the Prophet desires to take her in marriage (a privilege exclusively for you, not for the [rest of the] believers)...) [Al-Ahzab: 50].

The matter of the mutual imprecation in the verse is something that does not transcend its specific instance, which is the imprecation of the Prophet with the Christians. If there were no claimants realized in the plural description on both sides in that instance, the use of "the liars" (الكاذبين) in the plural form would not be sound at all.

**If you say:** Just as the Christians who came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) were possessors of a claim—which is that the Messiah is God, or the son of God, or the third of three, without any difference between them at all, nor between their women and their men in that regard—similarly, the claim on the side of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم)—which is that there is no god but Allah and that Jesus son of Mary is His servant and His messenger—was upheld by all the believers without any exclusivity to anyone among them, even the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). Thus, those he brought forth would have no merit over others, except that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) brought those he brought as a sample (انموذج) for what the verse included of sons, women, and souls. Furthermore, the claim (الدعوى) is different from the invitation (الدعوة), and you mentioned that they were partners in the invitation.

**I say:** If his bringing those he brought was by way of a sample, it would have been necessary to bring at least two men, several women, and three sons. Thus, the bringing of those he brought was only due to restriction (الانحصار), which is what validates the truthfulness of the fulfillment—meaning that he did not find anyone to fulfill the command of Him, the Exalted, in bringing them except those he brought, who were one man, one woman, and two sons.

If you were to reflect on the story, you would find that the delegation of Najran from the Christians only came to Medina to oppose the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and argue with him regarding the matter of Jesus son of Mary. The claim that he is the servant of Allah and His messenger was upheld by him, based on the revelation (الوحي) that he claimed for himself. As for the believers who followed him, the Christians had no business with them, nor did they have any desire to meet them, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, at the beginning of the verse: فمن حاجك فيه من بعد ما جاءك من العلم فقل (Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you, say...). Likewise, His saying, the Exalted—a few verses prior: فإن حاجوك فقل اسلمت وجهي لله ومن اتبعن (Should they argue with you, say, ‘I have surrendered my self to Allah, and [so has] he who follows me’...).

From here it becomes clear: that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) bringing those he brought for the imprecation was not by way of a sample, as the believers—by virtue of their mere faith—had no share in this disputation and imprecation such that they would be exposed to the curse and punishment alternating between them and their opponent. Rather, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) brought those he brought because he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was the party of the disputation and the invitation. Thus, it was his right to expose himself to the expected calamity in the event of lying. Had the claim not been upheld by those he brought just as it was upheld by his own noble self, there would have been no reason for him to bring them. Therefore, his bringing them was due to the restriction of those who uphold his claim—among the sons, women, and souls—to them, not by way of bringing a sample. Thus, it is correct that the claim was upheld by them just as it was upheld by him.

Furthermore, the Christians only sought him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) out not merely because he held the view that Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) was the servant of Allah and His messenger and believed that, but because he was claiming it and inviting them to it. Thus, the invitation (الدعوة) was the primary reason that prompted them to come and argue. Therefore, his presence and the presence of those who were with him for the imprecation was for the sake of both the claim and the invitation together. They were his partners in the religious invitation just as they shared with him in the claim, as we have mentioned.

**If you say:** Suppose his bringing them was because they were from him, and this description was restricted to them. However, it appears—as dictated by common custom—that a person bringing his loved ones and the "pieces of his liver" (أفلاذ كبده) from among women and children into dangers and horrors is proof of his confidence in safety, well-being, and protection. Thus, his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) bringing them does not indicate more than that. As for them being partners in the invitation, his action is far from indicating that.

**I say:** Yes, the beginning of the verse does not indicate more than what you mentioned. However, you have already learned that its conclusion—namely His saying: على الكاذبين (upon the liars)—indicates the certain realization of "liars" in one of the two sides of the disputation and imprecation. This cannot be fulfilled unless there is a group in each of the two sides that possesses a claim, whether truthful or lying. Therefore, those whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) brought were participants with him in the claim and in the invitation as previously stated. Thus, it is established that all those present were possessors of a claim and an invitation along with him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and were partners in that.

**If you say:** What you have mentioned necessitates their being partners in prophethood (النبوة).

**I say:** Not at all. It has been clarified in our previous discussions on prophethood that invitation (الدعوة) and transmission (التبليغ) are not identical to prophethood and the mission (البعثة), even if they are among its affairs and requirements, and among the divine offices and stations that one holds. Likewise, it has been clarified from the previous discussion on imamate (الإمامة) that they are not identical to imamate, even if they are among its requirements in a certain way.

**His saying, the Exalted:** إن هذا القصص الحق وما من إله إلا الله (This is indeed the true narrative, and there is no god but Allah). This is an allusion to the previously mentioned narrative of Jesus (peace be upon him). The speech contains a "reversal restriction" (قصر القلب) — *a rhetorical device used to correct a misconception by restricting the attribute to the subject contrary to what the listener believes* — meaning: what We have narrated is the truth, not what the Christians claim regarding the matter of Jesus.

In the use of *inna* (إن), the letter *lam* (ل), and the pronoun of separation (ضمير الفصل), there is an intense emphasis to comfort the soul of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and encourage him in the matter of the imprecation by awakening his attribute of certainty, insight, and trust in the revelation that Allah the Glorified sent down to him. This is followed by a second emphasis by presenting the reality through its necessary implication, which is His saying: وما من إله إلا الله (and there is no god but Allah), for this sentence is a necessary consequence of the aforementioned narrative being the truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وإن الله لهو العزيز الحكيم (and indeed Allah is the All-mighty, the All-wise) is coordinated with the beginning of the verse. With its intense emphasis, it is another comfort and encouragement for the soul of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) that Allah is not incapable of aiding the truth and supporting it, nor is He heedless or distracted from that through neglect or ignorance. For He is the All-mighty (العزيز) — *so He is not incapable of what He intends* — the All-wise (الحكيم) — *so He is not ignorant nor does He neglect* — not what the imaginations of the opponents of truth have fashioned of a god other than Allah the Glorified.

From here, the reason for using the two names, the All-mighty and the All-wise, becomes apparent, and that the speech is intended for reversal restriction (قصر القلب) or singular restriction (الإفراد).

**His saying, the Exalted:** فإن الله عليم بالمفسدين (then Allah indeed knows the agents of corruption). Since the purpose of the disputation, as well as the imprecation, is in reality the manifestation of the truth, it would be inconceivable for one who desires the goal and objective to turn away from the path. If they had intended by that the manifestation of the truth, while knowing that Allah the Glorified is the Guardian of the truth and does not accept its vanishing or refutation, they would not have turned away from it. If they turned away, it is only because they do not intend by the disputation the manifestation of the truth, but rather outward victory and the preservation of their current status and the tradition upon which their custom has solidified. They only desire what their whims and fancies beautify for them of the form of life, not the righteous life that accords with truth and felicity. Thus, they do not desire reform (إصلاح), but rather the corruption (إفساد) of the world by corrupting the felicitous life. Therefore, if they turn away, it is only because they are agents of corruption (مفسدون).

From here it appears that in the "recompense" (الجزاء), the cause was placed in the position of the effect—meaning corruption (الإفساد) in place of the lack of desire for the manifestation of the truth.

The recompense was imbued with the attribute of knowledge (العلم) where it was said: فإن الله عليم (then Allah indeed knows), then emphasized with *inna* (إن) to indicate that this attribute is realized in their souls and rooted in their hearts. This suggests that they will inevitably turn away from the imprecation; and they did so, confirming the word of Allah by their action.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): When the Christians of Najran came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and their leaders were al-Ahtam, al-Aqib, and al-Sayyid, the time for their prayer arrived. They began to strike the wooden clapper (الناقوس) and prayed. The companions of the Messenger of Allah said: "O Messenger of Allah, this in your mosque?" He said: "Leave them." When they finished, they approached the Messenger of Allah and said: "To what do you invite?" He said: "To the testimony that there is no god but Allah, and that I am the Messenger of Allah, and that Jesus is a created servant who eats, drinks, and excretes." They said: "Then who is his father?" Revelation then descended upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and He said: "Say to them: What do you say about Adam? Was he a created servant who ate, drank, excreted, and married?" The Prophet asked them, and they said: "Yes." He said: "Then who was his father?" They were confounded. Then Allah revealed: إن مثل عيسی عند الله كمثل آدم خلقه من تراب (Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust...) the verse, and His saying: فمن حاجك فيه من بعد ما جاءك من العلم (Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you...) until His saying: فنجعل لعنة الله علی الكاذبين (and call down Allah’s curse upon the liars). The Messenger of Allah said: "Then engage in mutual imprecation with me; if I am truthful, the curse shall be sent down upon you, and if I am a liar, it shall be sent down upon me." They said: "You have been fair." So they made an appointment for the imprecation. When they returned to their dwellings, their leaders—al-Sayyid, al-Aqib, and al-Ahtam—said: "If he imprecates against us with his people, we shall imprecate with him, for then he is not a prophet. But if he imprecates with his specific household (أهل بيته) only, we shall not imprecate with him, for he would not bring forth his household unless he were truthful." When morning came, they came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and with him were the Commander of the Faithful, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn (peace be upon them). The Christians said: "Who are these?" It was said to them: "This is his cousin, his successor (وصيه), and his son-in-law, Ali bin Abi Talib; this is his daughter Fatimah; and these are his two sons, al-Hasan and al-Husayn." They became afraid and said to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): "We give you our satisfaction, so excuse us from the imprecation." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) made peace with them on the condition of the tribute (الجزية) and they departed.

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain of narration from al-Rayyan bin al-Salt from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in his discussion with al-Ma'mun and the scholars regarding the difference between the Progeny (العترة) and the Nation (الامة), and the merit of the Progeny over the Nation. In it, the scholars said: "Has Allah explained 'the choosing' (الاصطفاء) in His Book?" Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: "He explained the choosing outwardly, besides the inward, in twelve places," and he mentioned the places from the Quran. He said regarding them: "As for the third, it was when Allah distinguished the pure ones from His creation and commanded His Prophet to engage in mutual imprecation with them in the verse of imprecation. He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: فمن حاجك فيه من بعد ما جاءك من العلم فقل تعالوا ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم ونسائنا ونسائكم وأنفسنا وأنفسكم (Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you, say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our souls and your souls’)." The scholars said: "He meant by it himself." Abu al-Hasan said: "You have erred. He only meant by it Ali bin Abi Talib. Among the proofs for this is the saying of the Prophet: 'The sons of Wali'ah must desist, or I shall surely send to them a man like my own soul (كنفسي),' meaning Ali bin Abi Talib. And He meant by 'the sons' al-Hasan and al-Husayn, and He meant by 'the women' Fatimah. This is a specialty in which no one precedes them, a merit which no human can reach, and an honor in which no creature can surpass them, as He made the soul of Ali like his own soul." The hadith.

From him, with its chain of narration to Imam Musa bin Ja'far (peace be upon them both) in a discussion of his with al-Rashid. Al-Rashid said to him: "How can you say: 'We are the progeny of the Prophet,' while the Prophet left no male offspring? Offspring (العقب) belongs to the male, not the female, and you are the children of the daughter, and she has no offspring." I said: "I ask him by the right of kinship, and the grave and the one in it, to excuse me from this question." He said: "You shall inform me of your proof regarding it, O son of Ali, and you, O Musa, are their leader (يعسوبهم) and the Imam of their time—so it has been reported to me. I shall not excuse you from anything I ask you until you bring me a proof for it from the Book of Allah. You, the assembly of the children of Ali, claim that nothing of it escapes you—not an *alif* nor a *waw*—but that its interpretation (تأويله) is with you, and you argue with His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: ما فرطنا في الكتاب من شيء (We have not omitted anything from the Book), and you have dispensed with the opinions of the scholars and their analogy (قياسهم)."

I said: "Do you permit me to answer?" He said: "Bring it." I said: "I seek refuge in Allah from the accursed Satan. In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. ومن ذريته داود وسليمان وأيوب ويوسف وموسی وهارون وكذلك نجزي المحسنين وزكريا ويحيى وعيسى وإلياس (...and of his descendants, David and Solomon, Job, Joseph, Moses and Aaron—thus do We reward the virtuous—and Zechariah, John, Jesus and Elias...) [Al-An'am: 84-85]. Who is the father of Jesus, O Commander of the Faithful?" He said: "He has no father." I said: "He only joined him to the descendants of the Prophets through Mary; likewise, Allah the Exalted joined us to the descendants of the Prophet through our mother Fatimah. Shall I give you more, O Commander of the Faithful?" He said: "Bring it." I said: "The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فمن حاجك فيه من بعد ما جاءك من العلم فقل تعالوا ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم ونسائنا ونسائكم وأنفسنا وأنفسكم ثم نبتهل فنجعل لعنتة الله علی الكاذبين (Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you, say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our souls and your souls; then let us pray earnestly and call down Allah’s curse upon the liars’). No one has claimed that the Prophet entered anyone under the cloak (الكساء) at the imprecation with the Christians except Ali bin Abi Talib, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn. Thus, the interpretation of His saying: 'our sons' was al-Hasan and al-Husayn, 'our women' was Fatimah, and 'our souls' was Ali bin Abi Talib."

In the inquiries of al-Ma'mun from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): Al-Ma'mun said: "What is the proof for the caliphate (خلافة) of your grandfather Ali bin Abi Talib?" He said: "The verse of 'our souls' (أنفسنا)." He said: "Were it not for 'our women' (نسائنا)." He said: "Were it not for 'our sons' (أبنائنا)."

**I say:** His saying: "The verse of 'our souls'" means that Allah made the soul of Ali like the soul of His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). His saying: "Were it not for 'our women'" means that the word "our women" in the verse is proof that the intended meaning of "souls" is men, so there would be no special merit in it then. His saying: "Were it not for 'our sons'" means that the presence of "our sons" in it indicates the contrary; for if the intended meaning of "souls" were men, there would be no place for the mention of "sons."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, with its chain of narration from Hariz from Imam Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) was asked about his virtues, so he mentioned some of them, then they said to him: "Give us more." He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was approached by two rabbis from the rabbis of the Christians from the people of Najran. They spoke regarding the matter of Jesus, so Allah revealed this verse: إن مثل عيسی عند الله كمثل آدم (Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam...) to the end of the verse. Then the Messenger of Allah entered and took the hand of Ali, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and Fatimah, then he came out and raised his palm to the sky, separated his fingers, and invited them to the mutual imprecation." He said: And Abu Ja'far (peace be upon them both) said: "Likewise, the imprecation is to interlock his hand in his hand and raise them both to the sky." When the two rabbis saw him, one of them said to his companion: "By Allah, if he is a prophet, we shall surely perish, and if he is other than a prophet, his people will suffice us." So they desisted and departed.

**I say:** This meaning, or what is close to it, is narrated in other narrations from the paths of the Shi'a. In all of them, those whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) brought for the imprecation were Ali, Fatimah, and the two Hasans. It was narrated by al-Shaykh in his *Amali* with his chain from Amir bin Sa'd from his father; he also narrated it therein with his chain from Abd al-Rahman bin Kathir from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him); he also narrated it therein with his chain from Salim bin Abi al-Ja'd, tracing it back to Abu Dharr (may Allah be pleased with him); he also narrated it therein with his chain from Rabi'ah bin Najid from Ali (peace be upon him); al-Mufid narrated it in the book *al-Ikhtisas* with his chain from Muhammad bin al-Zabarqan from Imam Musa bin Ja'far (peace be upon them both); he also narrated it therein from Muhammad bin al-Munkadir from his father from his grandfather; al-Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Muhammad bin Sa'id al-Urduni from Musa bin Muhammad bin al-Rida from his brother; he also narrated it from Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him); he also narrated it therein in another narration from al-Ahwal from him (peace be upon him), and from al-Mundhir from Ali (peace be upon him); he also narrated it therein with his chain from Amir bin Sa'd; al-Furat narrated it in his *Tafsir* with a connected chain from Abu Ja'far, and from Abu Rafi', al-Sha'bi, Ali (peace be upon him), and Shahr bin Hawshab; and it was narrated in *Rawdat al-Wa'izin*, *I'lam al-Wara*, *al-Khara'ij*, and others.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Tha'labi, from Mujahid and al-Kalbi: When he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) invited them to the imprecation, they said: "Wait until we return and consider." When they were alone, they said to al-Aqib—who was their man of counsel: "O Abd al-Masih, what do you see?" He said: "By Allah, you have certainly known, O assembly of Christians, that Muhammad is a sent prophet, and he has brought you the decisive word regarding the matter of your companion. By Allah, never has a people imprecated against a prophet such that their elders lived or their young grew up. If you do it, we shall surely perish. If you refuse anything but adherence to your religion and staying upon what you are, then make peace with the man and depart to your lands."

So they came to the Messenger of Allah, and he had gone out embracing al-Husayn, holding the hand of al-Hasan, with Fatimah walking behind him and Ali behind her, while he was saying: "When I supplicate, say 'Amen'." The Bishop of Najran said: "O assembly of Christians! Indeed, I see faces such that if they were to ask Allah to remove a mountain from its place, He would remove it by them. So do not imprecate, lest you perish and not a single Christian remain on the face of the earth until the Day of Resurrection." They said: "O Abu al-Qasim, we have decided not to imprecate with you, and to leave you upon your religion and remain upon our religion." He said: "If you refuse the imprecation, then submit to Islam; you shall have what the Muslims have and be bound by what they are bound by." They refused. He said: "Then I shall wage war against you." They said: "We have no strength for war with the Arabs, but we shall make peace with you on the condition that you do not raid us, nor frighten us, nor turn us from our religion, provided that we pay to you every year two thousand garments: a thousand in Safar and a thousand in Rajab, and thirty coats of mail of common iron." So he made peace with them on that.

And he said: "By the One in Whose hand is my soul, destruction had dangled over the people of Najran. Had they engaged in mutual cursing, they would have been transformed into apes and swine, and the valley would have blazed with fire upon them, and Allah would have uprooted Najran and its people, even the birds upon the tops of the trees. Before the year had passed, all the Christians would have perished."

**I say:** The story was narrated similarly in the book *al-Maghazi* from Ibn Ishaq; al-Maliki also narrated it in *al-Fusul al-Muhimmah* from the commentators similarly; and al-Hamawi narrated it from Ibn Jurayh similarly.

His saying: "a thousand in Safar"—the meaning is Muharram, which is the beginning of the year for the Arabs. It used to be called Safar in the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية); it was said "the first Safar" and "the second Safar." The Arabs used to practice intercalation (النسيء) in the first Safar, then Islam established the sanctity in the first Safar, so it was named for that reason "the Month of Allah, al-Muharram," then it became famous as al-Muharram.

In *Sahih Muslim*, from Amir bin Sa'd bin Abi Waqqas from his father, he said: Mu'awiyah bin Abi Sufyan commanded Sa'd and said: "What prevents you from reviling Abu Turab?" He said: "As for the three things I remember that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) said, I shall never revile him. For me to possess even one of them is more beloved to me than red camels. I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) saying when he left him behind in one of his raids, and Ali said to him: 'O Messenger of Allah, have you left me behind with the women and children?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) said to him: 'Are you not pleased to be to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me?' And I heard him saying on the day of Khaybar: 'I shall surely give the standard tomorrow to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger, and whom Allah and His Messenger love.' He said: 'So we craned our necks for it.' He said: 'Call Ali for me.' He was brought with sore eyes, so he spat in his eyes and handed the standard to him, and Allah granted victory at his hands. And when this verse was revealed: قل تعالوا ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم ونسائنا ونسائكم وأنفسنا وأنفسكم ثم نبتهل (say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our souls and your souls; then let us pray earnestly...'), the Messenger of Allah called Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn and said: 'O Allah, these are the People of my House (أهل بيتي).'"

**I say:** It was narrated by al-Tirmidhi in his *Sahih*; Abu al-Mu'ayyad al-Muwaffaq bin Ahmad narrated it in the book *Fada'il Ali*; Abu Nu'aym also narrated it in *al-Hilyah* from Amir bin Sa'd from his father; and al-Hamawayni narrated it in the book *Fara'id al-Simtayn*.

In *Hilyat al-Awliya* by Abu Nu'aym, with its chain from Amir bin Abi Waqqas from his father, he said: When this verse was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) called Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn and said: "O Allah, these are the People of my House."

In it, with its chain from al-Sha'bi from Jabir, he said: Al-Aqib and al-Tayyib came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) and he invited them to Islam. They said: "We have submitted, O Muhammad." He said: "You have lied. If you wish, I shall inform you of what prevents you from Islam." They said: "Then bring it to us." He said: "Love of the cross, drinking wine, and eating the flesh of swine." Jabir said: "So he invited them to mutual cursing, and they made an appointment with him to come in the morning. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) went out in the morning and took the hand of Ali, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and Fatimah, and sent for them, but they refused to answer him and acknowledged him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'By the One Who sent me with the truth, had they done so, the valley would have rained fire upon them'." Jabir said: "Regarding them was revealed: ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم (Let us call our sons and your sons)." Jabir said: "'Our souls and your souls' were the Messenger of Allah and Ali, 'our sons' were al-Hasan and al-Husayn, and 'our women' was Fatimah."

**I say:** It was narrated by Ibn al-Maghazili in his *Manaqib* with his chain from al-Sha'bi from Jabir; al-Hamawayni also narrated it in *Fara'id al-Simtayn* with his chain from him; al-Maliki narrated it in *al-Fusul al-Muhimmah* as a detached report (مرسلاً) from him; he also narrated it from Abu Dawud al-Tayalisi from Shu'bah from al-Sha'bi as a detached report; and it was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Hakim, who authenticated it, and from Ibn Mardawayh and Abu Nu'aym in *al-Dala'il* from Jabir.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Nu'aym recorded in *al-Dala'il* through the path of al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas: The delegation of Najran from the Christians came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and they were fourteen men from their nobles, among them al-Sayyid, who was the elder, and al-Aqib, who was the one after him and their man of counsel. Then he recounted the story similar to what has passed.

In it also, al-Bayhaqi recorded in *al-Dala'il* through the path of Salamah bin Abd Yashu' from his father from his grandfather: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) wrote to the people of Najran before the *Ta-Sin* of Solomon was revealed to him: "In the Name of Allah, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. From Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah, to the Bishop of Najran and the people of Najran. If you submit, I praise Allah to you, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. To proceed: I invite you to the worship of Allah from the worship of servants, and I invite you to the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah from the guardianship of servants. If you refuse, then the tribute (الجزية); and if you refuse, then I have declared war upon you. Peace." When the Bishop read the letter, he was terrified and struck with intense fear. He sent for a man from the people of Najran called Shurahbil bin Wada'ah and handed him the letter of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). He read it, and the Bishop said to him: "What is your opinion?" Shurahbil said: "You have known what Allah promised Abraham regarding the descendants of Ishmael of prophethood, so what is the security that this man is not he? I have no opinion regarding prophethood; if it were an opinion regarding a worldly matter, I would have advised you on it and exerted myself for you." So the Bishop sent for one after another of the people of Najran, and all of them said the same as Shurahbil. Their opinion converged on sending Shurahbil bin Wada'ah, Abdullah bin Shurahbil, and Jabbar bin Fayd so they might bring them news of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم).

The delegation set out until they came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). He questioned them and they questioned him, and the questioning continued between him and them until they said to him: "What do you say about Jesus son of Mary?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: "I have nothing regarding him this day of mine, so stay until I inform you of what is said regarding Jesus tomorrow morning." Then Allah revealed this verse: إن مثل عيسی عند الله كمثل آدم خلقه من تراب (Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust...) until His saying: فنجعل لعنة الله علی الكاذبين (and call down Allah’s curse upon the liars). They refused to acknowledge that. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) rose the next morning after informing them of the news, he approached embracing al-Hasan and al-Husayn in a cloak (خميلة) of his, with Fatimah walking behind his back for the mutual cursing—and he had several wives on that day. Shurahbil said to his two companions: "Indeed, I see a matter approaching; if this man is a sent prophet and we imprecate against him, not a hair nor a nail of us will remain on the face of the earth but that it will perish." They said to him: "What is your opinion?" He said: "My opinion is that I make him the arbiter, for I see a man who will never judge unjustly." They said to him: "That is up to you." So Shurahbil met the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and said: "I have seen something better than imprecating against you." He said: "And what is it?" He said: "Your judgment today until the night, and your night until the morning; whatever you judge regarding us is permissible." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) returned and did not imprecate against them, and he made peace with them on the condition of the tribute.

In it, Ibn Jarir recorded from Ilba' bin Ahmar al-Yashkuri, who said: When this verse was revealed: قل تعالوا ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم (say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons...’) the verse, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) sent for Ali, Fatimah, and their two sons al-Hasan and al-Husayn, and invited the Jews to imprecate against them. A young man from the Jews said: "Woe to you! Do you not remember yesterday your brothers who were transformed into apes and swine? Do not imprecate." So they desisted.

**I say:** The narration supports that the pronoun in His saying, the Exalted: فمن حاجك فيه (Should anyone argue with you concerning him), refers to "the truth" in His saying: الحق من ربك (This is the truth from your Lord). Thus, the ruling of the imprecation is completed for other than specifically Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him). This would then be another story occurring after the story of inviting the delegation of Najran to the imprecation, according to what the many, widely-transmitted reports—most of which were mentioned previously—recount.

Ibn Tawus said in the book *Sa'd al-Su'ud*: I saw in the book *Tafsir ma Nazala min al-Qur'an fi al-Nabi wa Ahli Baytihi* (Exegesis of what was revealed of the Quran regarding the Prophet and his Household) authored by Muhammad bin al-Abbas bin Marwan: that he narrated the report of the imprecation through fifty-one paths from those he named among the companions and others. He counted among them al-Hasan bin Ali (peace be upon them both), Uthman bin Affan, Sa'd bin Abi Waqqas, Bakr bin Simal, Talhah, al-Zubayr, Abd al-Rahman bin Awf, Abdullah bin Abbas, Abu Rafi' the client of the Prophet, Jabir bin Abdullah, al-Bara' bin Azib, and Anas bin Malik.

This was narrated in *al-Manaqib* from several narrators and commentators, as well as by al-Suyuti in *al-Durr al-Manthur*.

Among the strange remarks is what some commentators have mentioned, where he said: "The narrations are in agreement that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) chose for the imprecation Ali, Fatimah, and his two sons, and they interpret the word 'our women' as Fatimah, and the word 'our souls' as Ali only. The sources of these narrations are the Shi'a, and their objective in them is known. They have exerted themselves in promoting them as much as they could until they became popular among many of the Sunnis. However, those who fabricated them did not apply them well to the verse, for the word 'our women' is not said by an Arab intending his daughter, especially if he has wives, and this is not understood from their language. Even more remote than that is for 'our souls' to mean Ali. Furthermore, the delegation of Najran—regarding whom it is said the verse was revealed—did not have their women and children with them. All that is understood from the verse is the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) to invite those arguing and disputing regarding Jesus from the People of the Book to a gathering of men, women, and children, and he gathers the believers—men, women, and children—and they pray earnestly to Allah the Exalted to curse the liar in what he says about Jesus.

This request indicates the strength of the certainty of its possessor and his confidence in what he says, just as the refusal of those from the People of the Book who were invited to that—whether they were the Christians of Najran or others—indicates their skepticism in their arguments and their disputations in what they say, and their wavering in what they believe, and their being upon other than clear proof or certainty. How can one who believes in Allah accept that this assembly of people—the truthful and the false—gather in one place, turning to Allah in request of His curse and removal from His mercy? What audacity against Allah and mockery of His power and greatness is stronger than this?"

He said: "As for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and the believers being upon certainty regarding what they believed about Jesus (peace be upon him), His saying, the Exalted, is sufficient for us in explaining it: من بعد ما جاءك من العلم (after the knowledge that has come to you). Knowledge in these matters of belief is not intended to mean anything but certainty. In His saying: ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم (let us call our sons and your sons) etc., there are two views:

First: That each party invites the other, so you invite our sons, and we invite your sons; and so on for the rest.

Second: That each party invites its own family, so we the Muslims invite our sons, our women, and our souls; and you likewise.

There is no problem in either of the two views of distribution in the invitation of the 'souls'. The problem only lies in it according to the view of the Shi'a and those who followed them in the view of specification (التخصيص)." End quote.

**I say:** This speech—and I think the observer of it would almost suspect us in attributing it to the likes of him, and the intelligent person would not accept depositing it and its likes in scholarly books—we have only cited it despite its weakness and collapse so that it may be known where bias and sectarianism (العصبية) lead their possessor in the collapse of understanding and the wretchedness of insight. He destroys everything he built upon and builds everything he destroyed, and he does not care; and because evil must be known so that it may be avoided.

The discussion is in two stations: First: the indication of the verse on the superiority of Ali (peace be upon him), which is a theological (كلامي) discussion outside the purpose for which this book was established—which is the consideration of the meanings of the Quranic verses.

**Second:** The investigation of what this speaker mentioned in terms of its relation to the meaning of the verse of imprecation, and the narrations reported regarding what occurred between the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and the delegation of Najran; and this is an exegetical (تفسيري) investigation within our purpose.

You have already learned what the verse indicates, and that what we have cited of the numerous, widely-transmitted reports is what corresponds to the meaning of the verse. By reflecting on that, the aspects of fallacy in this fabricated argument and the weak view that leads to no result become clear. Here is their detail:

**Among them:** His saying: "The sources of these narrations are the Shi'a... they have exerted themselves in promoting them as much as they could until they became popular among many of the Sunnis," after his saying: "The narrations are in agreement." I wish I knew which narrations he means by this saying? Does he mean these widely-transmitted narrations which the traditionists (المحدثون) have unanimously agreed to transmit and not discard? They are not one, two, or three; the people of hadith have unanimously agreed on their transmission and their acceptance, and the masters of the collections have established them in their collections, among them Muslim in his *Sahih* and al-Tirmidhi in his *Sahih*, and the people of history have supported them.

Then the commentators unanimously agreed on citing them and depositing them in their exegeses without objection or doubt, and among them is a group from the people of hadith and history like al-Tabari, Abu al-Fida' bin Kathir, al-Suyuti, and others.

Then, who does he mean by the Shi'a who are the sources for these narrations? Does he mean those at whom the chains of transmission (سلاسل الأسناد) in the narrations end—namely Sa'd bin Abi Waqqas, Jabir bin Abdullah, Abdullah bin Abbas, and other companions? Or the successors (التابعين) who transmitted from them by taking and narrating, like Abu Salih, al-Kalbi, al-Suddi, al-Sha'bi, and others, and that they became Shi'a for transmitting what does not please his whim? These and their likes and peers are the intermediaries in transmitting the Sunnah (السنة), and with their rejection, no mentioned Sunnah nor narrated biography (سيرة) remains. How can it be possible for a Muslim or a researcher—even from those who do not profess Islam—to nullify the Sunnah and then seek to become acquainted with the details of what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) brought of teaching and legislation? The Quran is vocal about the authority of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) word and his biography, and vocal about the survival of the religion in its vitality. If the nullification of the Sunnah were permissible from the root, no trace would remain for the Quran, nor any fruit for its revelation.

Or does he mean that the Shi'a inserted these hadiths into the collections of hadith and books of history? Then the peril of the collapse of the Sunnah and the nullification of the Sharia (الشريعة) would return; rather, the affliction would be more general and the corruption more complete.

**Among them:** His saying: "and they interpret the word 'our women' as Fatimah, and the word 'our souls' as Ali only." His intent by this is that they say the word "our women" was used and Fatimah was intended by it, and likewise the intended meaning of the word "our souls" was Ali only. It is as if he understood it from what some of the previous narrations contain: "Jabir said: 'our women' was Fatimah and 'our souls' was Ali," the report. He has misunderstood; for the intended meaning in the verse by the term "our women" is not Fatimah, nor by the term "our souls" is it Ali. Rather, the meaning is that since he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) did not bring in the station of fulfillment (الامتثال) except her and him, that revealed that she is the individual instance (المصداق الفرد) for "our women," and that he is the sole instance for "our souls," and that they both are the instance for "our sons." The intended meaning of "the sons," "the women," and "the souls" in the verse was "the family" (الأهل); thus they are the People of the House of the Messenger of Allah and his specific ones, as reported in some narrations after the mention of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) bringing them that he said: "O Allah, these are the People of my House." For the meaning of the sentence is: "I did not find anyone to call besides these."

What we have mentioned of the intent is indicated by what occurred in some narrations: "'our souls and your souls' were the Messenger of Allah and Ali." For the wording is explicit that the objective is the explanation of the instance (المصداق) rather than the meaning of the term.

**Among them:** His saying: "However, those who fabricated them did not apply them well to the verse, for the word 'our women' is not said by an Arab intending his daughter, especially if he has wives, and this is not understood from their language. Even more remote than that is for 'our souls' to mean Ali." This strange meaning that he imagined is what necessitated his discarding these narrations despite their plurality, then attacking their narrators and everyone who received them with acceptance, and accusing them of what he mentioned. It was his duty to be mindful of his position regarding the interpretation of the Book, and to remember this vast multitude of the leaders of eloquence (البلاغة) and masters of exposition (البيان), who cited them in their exegeses and other works without any hesitation or objection.

Here is the author of *al-Kashshaf*—and he is the one who perhaps corrected the leaders of recitation in their recitations—saying at the end of the exegesis of the verse: "In it is a proof, than which nothing is stronger, for the merit of the People of the Cloak (أصحاب الكساء) (peace be upon them). In it is a clear proof for the correctness of the prophethood of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), because no one—neither supporter nor opponent—has narrated that they answered to that." End quote.

How then did it escape these great men, the heroes of eloquence and the knights of literature, that these reports—despite their plurality and repetition in the collections of hadith—attribute to the Quran that it errs in its exposition by using "women" (which is a plural) in the case of a single person?

No, by my life! Rather, the matter became confused for this speaker, and he mistook the concept (المفهوم) for the instance (المصداق). He imagined: that if Allah, exalted is His Name, said to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): فمن حاجك فيه من بعد ما جاءك من العلم فقل تعالوا ندع أبنائنا وأبنائكم (Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you, say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons...’) etc., and it was correct that those arguing at the time of the verse's revelation were the delegation of Najran—and they were fourteen men according to some narrations who did not have women or sons with them; and it was also correct that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) went out to their imprecation and there was no one with him except Ali, Fatimah, and the two Hasans—the necessity of that would be that the meaning of "whoever argues" is the delegation of Najran, and the meaning of "our women" is the single woman, and the meaning of "our souls" is the single soul, and "your women" and "your sons" remained without meaning since there were no women or sons with the delegation!

He should have added to that the necessity of using "sons" (أبناء)—which is a plural—for the dual (التثنية), which is more hideous than using the plural for the singular. For the use of the plural for the singular is sometimes found in the speech of the later poets (المولدين) even if it is not found in original Arabic except in speaking for the purpose of magnification (التعظيم); but the use of the plural for the dual is something for which there is no justification at all.

This is what prompted him to discard the narrations and accuse them of fabrication, and it is not as he imagined.

The clarification of that is that in eloquent speech (الكلام البليغ), one only follows what the station (المقام) necessitates of revealing what is important to reveal. Sometimes the station is a station of address between two interlocutors or two tribes, each of whom denies or is ignorant of the state of his companion. Thus, the speech is placed upon what nature and custom necessitate, so it is expressed in a way that suits that. If one of two disputing tribes wants to inform its companion that the dispute and defense are upheld by all the persons of its tribe—males and females, young and old—it says: "We dispute with you" or "We fight you with the men, the women (الظعائن), and the children." Thus, it places the speech upon what nature and custom necessitate, for custom necessitates that a tribe of people has women and children. The objective is related to explaining to the opponent that they are one hand against whoever disputes with them and they dispute with him. If it were said: "We dispute with you or fight you with the men, the women, and two sons of ours," it would be informing of a matter additional to the necessity of the station, requiring additional care and introducing to the opponent for an additional subtle point.

As for the custom among acquaintances, friends, and close companions, speech is often structured according to natural disposition (الطبع) and habit (العادة). Thus, in an invitation to a banquet or a gathering, one might say: "We shall attend with our own selves (أنفسنا), our women, and our children." At times, one might speak more expansively in introducing others, saying: "We shall serve you with the men, the daughter, and the two young grandsons," and the like.

Therefore, natural disposition, habit, and the apparent state of affairs (ظاهر الحال) have their own governing rule, while the actual reality (واقع الأمر) and the external essence (خارج العين) have another. These two may sometimes differ. Thus, if someone bases his speech on recounting what is known from his apparent state—as dictated by disposition and habit—and then the true reality and actual state of affairs emerge contrary to what he recounted of his apparent state, he is not considered to have erred in his speech, nor to have lied in his report, nor to have been frivolous or jesting in his saying (قوله).

The verse follows this very course. His saying, the Exalted: **فَقُلْ تَعَالَوْا نَدْعُ أَبْنَاءَنَا وَأَبْنَاءَكُمْ وَنِسَاءَنَا وَنِسَاءَكُمْ وَأَنْفُسَنَا وَأَنْفُسَكُمْ** "say, ‘Come! Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our souls and your souls...’" [Qarai 3:61] was intended—based on what has preceded—to mean: "Invite them so that you may attend with your elite kin who share with you in the claim and the knowledge, and they may attend with their elite kin." Then, the speech was structured according to what the apparent state of affairs suggests: that the Messenger of Allah has men, women, and sons among his kin, and they likewise have men, women, and sons among theirs. This is the requirement of the apparent state and the judgment of disposition and habit regarding him and them. As for the actual reality and its truth, it was that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) had no one from the men, women, and sons except for one soul (نفس), one daughter (بنت), and two sons (ابنان), while they had only men without women or sons. For this reason, when he came to them with one man, one woman, and two children, they did not confront him with accusations of error or falsehood. Nor did they excuse themselves from attending by saying, "You ordered the bringing of women and sons, and we have no women or sons," nor did anyone who narrated the story dismiss it as a fabrication or a deception.

From this, the invalidity of what he [the critic] cited becomes clear, where he said: "Furthermore, the delegation of Najran, regarding whom it is said the verse was revealed, did not have women or children with them."

**Among his points:** His saying: "All that is understood from the verse is the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) to invite those who were arguing and disputing regarding Jesus from the People of the Book to a gathering of men, women, and children, and that he should gather the believers—men, women, and children—and they should supplicate (يبتهلون) to Allah to curse the liar in what he says about Jesus..."—until his saying: "And how could anyone who believes in Allah agree to such a gathering of people, both the truthful and the false, in one place, turning toward Allah the Exalted to ask for His curse and banishment from His mercy? What greater audacity against Allah and mockery of His power and majesty could there be than this?"

The summary of his argument is that the verse invites both parties to gather with their souls, their women, and their progeny in one place and then supplicate for mutual imprecation (المباهلة). It is necessary to clarify: what is this gathering to which they were invited?

Is it a gathering of both parties in their entirety—meaning all the believers, who at that time (1) were the Arabs of Rabi‘ah and Mudar, most or all of them from Yemen, Hijaz, Iraq, and elsewhere; and the Christians, who were the people of Najran from Yemen, the Christians of the Levant, the Mediterranean coasts, the people of Rome, the Franks, the English, the Austrians, and others?

__________________
(1) This was in the ninth year [A.H.] according to some historians, or the tenth according to others, though none of these dates are free from difficulty, as will be discussed in the traditional research (البحث الروائي) concerning the verses following these.

These masses in the east and west of the earth, including men, women, and progeny, numbered in the millions upon millions at that time. No person of intellect doubts that it is impossible for them to gather in a single place. Ordinary causes (الأسباب العادية) reject this in all their aspects. The necessary consequence of this would be that the Quran is summoning people to the impossible (المحال), and making the manifestation of its proof and the clarification of the truth it claims dependent upon something that can never happen. This would have been an excuse—and what an excuse!—for the Christians in not responding to the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) invitation to the *mubahalah*, and it would have been more detrimental to his claim than to theirs.

Or is it a gathering of those present from both parties and those who fall under their ruling—meaning the believers from the people of Medina and its surroundings, and the people of Najran and those near them? This, while less absurd than the previous view, is just as impossible in terms of realization and occurrence as the former. Who at that time could have gathered the people of Medina and Najran in their entirety, even their women and progeny, in one place for mutual imprecation? Is such an invitation anything other than making the matter contingent upon the impossible, and an admission that the truth is unable to manifest?

Or is it a gathering of those directly involved in the dispute and argument from both parties—meaning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and those believers present with him, and the delegation of Najran from the Christians? In that case, his own objection applies: "Furthermore, the delegation of Najran, regarding whom it is said the verse was revealed, did not have their women or children with them," and this would fall into the very problem he mentioned.

**Among his points:** His saying: "As for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and the believers being in a state of certainty (يقين) regarding what they believed about Jesus (عليه‌السلام), it suffices us to cite the saying of the Exalted: 'after the knowledge that has come to you.' Knowledge (العلم) in these matters of creed (الاعتقادية) refers to nothing but certainty."

**I say:** As for knowledge therein meaning certainty, that is correct. But as for the verse indicating that the believers were in a state of certainty regarding the matter of Jesus (عليه‌السلام), I wonder from where he derives the proof for that? The verse, in its literal wording (**فَمَنْ حَاجَّكَ فِيهِ مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَكَ** "Should anyone argue with you concerning him, after the knowledge that has come to you..."), addresses only the status of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). The context of the address also does not include anyone other than him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) from among the believers, for the Christian delegation had no dispute or argument except with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم); they had no desire to meet the believers, nor did they speak a word to them, nor did the believers speak a word to them.

Yes, if the verse indicated the attainment of knowledge for anyone other than the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), it would indicate it for those who were brought for the *mubahalah*, based on what we derived from the saying of the Exalted: "upon the liars," as mentioned previously.

Rather, the Quran indicates that knowledge and certainty were not general to all the believers, as the Exalted says: **وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ** "And most of them do not believe in Allah without being polytheists." [Qarai 12:106]. He described them as being in polytheism (الشرك), and how can polytheism coexist with certainty? And the Exalted says: **وَإِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنَافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ مَّا وَعَدَنَا اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا** "And when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness were saying, ‘Allah and His Apostle did not promise us [anything] but delusion.’" [Qarai 33:12]. And the Exalted says: **وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَوْلَا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ ۖ فَإِذَا أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُّحْكَمَةٌ وَذُكِرَ فِيهَا الْقِتَالُ ۙ رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ نَظَرَ الْمَغْشِيِّ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْمَوْتِ ۖ فَأَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ طَاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ ۚ فَإِذَا عَزَمَ الْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا اللَّهَ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ** "The faithful say, ‘Why has not a surah been sent down?’ But when a definitive surah is sent down and fighting is mentioned in it, you see those in whose hearts is a sickness looking at you with the look of someone fainting at the prospect of death. So woe to them! Obedience and a well-spoken word [would be more suitable for them]. Then, when the matter is resolved, if they are true to Allah, it will surely be better for them." [Qarai 47:20-21]—until He said: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمَىٰ أَبْصَارَهُمْ** "They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He made them deaf, and blinded their eyes." [Qarai 47:23]. Thus, certainty is not realized except by some of the people of insight (أولي البصيرة) from the followers of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). The Exalted said: **فَإِنْ حَاجُّوكَ فَقُلْ أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّهِ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِ** "Should they argue with you, say, ‘I have submitted my will to Allah, and [so has] he who follows me.’" [Qarai 3:20], and He said: **قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي** "Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me.’" [Qarai 12:108].

**Among his points:** His saying: "Regarding His saying: 'Let us call our sons and your sons...', there are two possibilities: one is that each party calls the other..." You have already seen the invalidity of this first possibility and its lack of correspondence with the wording of the verse. For you have known that the purpose would have been fully achieved had it been said: "Come, let us supplicate and place the curse of Allah upon the liars." The addition of His saying: "Let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, and our souls and your souls" was only to indicate the necessity of each party bringing the dearest and most beloved things to them at the *mubahalah*, which are the sons, the women, and the souls (the family and the elite kin). This is only consistent if the meaning of the verse is: "We shall call our sons, our women, and our souls, and you shall call your sons, your women, and your souls, then we shall supplicate." However, if the meaning were: "We shall call your sons, your women, and your souls, and you shall call our sons, our women, and our souls, then we shall supplicate," the aforementioned purpose would be nullified.

Furthermore, this meaning is inherently unacceptable to a sound disposition. What would be the meaning of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) giving the Christians authority over his sons and women, and asking them to give him authority over their progeny and women so they could call one another to complete the attendance and *mubahalah*, when this could be achieved by each party calling their own family?

Moreover, understanding this meaning from the verse would require understanding the concept of "giving authority" (التسليط) or something similar—as mentioned—from it, and how could we understand that? The truth is that this possibility is discarded, and the other possibility—that the meaning is each party calling their own family—is the only one determined.

**Among his points:** His saying: "There is no difficulty in either of the two ways of distribution regarding the calling of the 'souls' (الأنفس); the difficulty only lies in the view of the Shi‘a and those who agree with them on the view of specification (التخصيص)." By "difficulty," he refers to the objection raised against the verse regarding the necessity of a person calling himself. This difficulty is not related to either of the two possibilities at all; rather, it is an objection to the view that the meaning of "our souls" (أنفسنا) is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) himself, as is recounted from some sectarian debates where one opponent claimed that "our souls" meant the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and it was objected that this would necessitate a person calling himself, which is invalid—a point alluded to in the second narration from *al-Uyun* mentioned previously.

From here, the fallaciousness of his saying: "the difficulty only lies in the view of the Shi‘a" becomes apparent. For their view, as we have presented, is that the meaning of "our souls" is the men from the Household (أهل بيت) of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), who, in terms of external application (المصداق), were the Messenger of Allah and Ali (عليهما الصلوة والسلام), and there is no difficulty in some of them calling others.

Thus, there is no difficulty for them, even according to what he attributed to them in his assumption: that the meaning of "our souls" is Ali. For there is no difficulty in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) calling Ali (عليه السلام).

His student [Rashid Rida] said in *al-Manar*, after referring to the narrations: "Ibn Asakir narrated from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, from his father: '**قُلْ تَعَالَوْا نَدْعُ أَبْنَاءَنَا وَأَبْنَاءَكُمْ**' (the verse); he said: 'So he came with Abu Bakr and his son, Umar and his son, and Uthman and his son.' He [the student] said: 'The apparent meaning is that the speech concerns the community of believers.'"

Then he said, after quoting the previously mentioned words of his teacher: "In the verse, you see the ruling of the participation of women with men in gathering for national competition and religious struggle. This is based on considering the woman like the man even in public affairs, except for what has been excluded..."—until the end of his lengthy discourse.

**I say:** As for the narration he mentioned, it is an anomalous (شاذة) narration that contradicts all the narrations of the verse despite their abundance and fame. The commentators have turned away from this narration. Furthermore, it contains that which does not correspond to reality, which is his claim that each of those mentioned had a son. At that time, none of them had a son at all.

It seems that by his saying, "The apparent meaning is that the speech concerns the community of believers," he wishes to derive from the narration an indication that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) brought all the believers and their children. Thus, the saying "So he came with Abu Bakr and his son..." would be a metaphor for his bringing the general body of believers. It appears he intends by this to support his teacher in the meaning he mentioned. You can see the state of this narration in terms of its anomaly, its rejection, its content, and its lack of indication for the meaning he mentioned.

As for what he mentioned regarding the verse's indication of the participation of women with men in public rights, if what he mentioned were correct, it would also indicate the participation of children. This alone is sufficient to invalidate what he mentioned.

We have already presented the discussion on their participation with men when discussing the verses of divorce in the second volume of this book, and a portion will follow in its appropriate place without the need for such derivations as he took from the verse.
